class CustomTimeDialog(simpledialog.Dialog):
    def body(self, master):
        self.timebutton_style = {"font": ("consolas", 11), 
        "fg": "white", "bg": "#3c3c3c", "relief": "flat"}
        self.title('Custom Time')
        self.time_background = "#242424"
        self.time_fg="white"

        master.configure(bg=self.time_background)
        self.configure(bg=self.time_background)

        self.ico = self.resource_path_timer("icon.ico")
        self.iconbitmap(self.ico)

        #timebox--------------------------------------------------
        ctime_frame = tk.Frame(master, bg=self.time_background)
        ctime_frame.grid(row=1, column=1)
        self.timebox = tk.Listbox(ctime_frame, height=6, width=15, font=self.timebutton_style["font"], bg=self.timebutton_style["bg"], fg="#dddddd", selectbackground="#222222")

        for time_string in ['15s', '30s', '45s', '1m', '5m', '10m']:
            self.timebox.insert(tk.END, time_string)
        self.timebox.grid(row=1, column=1)
        self.timebox.bind('<<ListboxSelect>>', self.set_spinboxes_from_selection)

        #minutebox-----------------------------------------------
        # Main frame to contain the minutes and seconds frames
        time_frame = tk.Frame(ctime_frame, bg=self.time_background)
        time_frame.grid(row=2, column=1, pady=5)# side=tk.TOP, fill=tk.X, padx=5, pady=5)

        # Create a frame for the minutes spinbox and label
        minutes_frame = tk.Frame(time_frame, bg=self.time_background)
        minutes_frame.pack(side=tk.LEFT, fill=tk.X)#, padx=5)

        tk.Label(minutes_frame, text="Minutes:", bg=self.time_background, fg=self.time_fg, font=self.timebutton_style["font"]).pack(side=tk.TOP)
        self.spin_minutes = tk.Spinbox(minutes_frame, from_=0, to=59, width=5, font=self.timebutton_style["font"])
        self.spin_minutes.pack(side=tk.TOP)

        # Create a frame for the seconds spinbox and label
        seconds_frame = tk.Frame(time_frame, bg=self.time_background)
        seconds_frame.pack(side=tk.LEFT, fill=tk.X, padx=5)

        tk.Label(seconds_frame, text="Seconds:", bg=self.time_background, fg=self.time_fg, font=self.timebutton_style["font"]).pack(side=tk.TOP)
        self.spin_seconds = tk.Spinbox(seconds_frame, from_=0, to=59, value=15, width=5, font=self.timebutton_style["font"], wrap=True)
        self.spin_seconds.pack(side=tk.TOP)

        #togsessbox-------------------------------------------------
        session = tk.LabelFrame(master, fg=self.timebutton_style["fg"], bg=self.time_background, relief=tk.FLAT)#, text="Sessions"
        session.grid(row=1, column=2)

        #togsessbox = tk.Frame(master, bg=self.time_background)
        togsess_frame = tk.Frame(session, bg=self.time_background)
        togsess_frame.grid (row=3,column=1, pady=5)
        self.togsess_mode = tk.BooleanVar(value=False)

        #self.togsess_frame = tk.Checkbutton(togsess_frame, bg=self.time_background)
        self.togsess_checkbox = tk.Checkbutton(togsess_frame, text="Session", 
        variable=self.togsess_mode, command=self.toggle_togsess)
        self.togsess_checkbox.configure(**self.timebutton_style, bd=0)
        self.togsess_checkbox.grid(row=1, column=1, sticky=tk.E, ipadx=12)

        self.img_togsess = tk.Spinbox(togsess_frame, width=3, from_=0, to=100, 
        font=self.timebutton_style["font"], wrap=True, x=500, value=5, state = 'disabled')
        self.img_togsess.grid(row=1, column=2)

        #sessionsbox-----------------------------------------------------------
        self.sessionlist = ['5 pics for 30s', '5 pics for 1m', '5 pics for 5m']
        self.sessionString = tk.StringVar(value=self.sessionlist)
        self.sessionbox = tk.Listbox(session, height=6, width=18, font=self.timebutton_style["font"], bg=self.timebutton_style["bg"], listvariable=self.sessionString, borderwidth=0, state = 'disabled', fg="#dddddd", selectbackground="#222222")#, fg=self.timebutton_style["fg"])       

        self.sessionbox.grid(row=1, column=1)

        #session buttons frame------------------------------------------------------------------
        sessionbuttons = tk.Frame(session, bg=self.time_background)
        sessionbuttons.grid(row=2, column=1)
        self.add_button = tk.Button(sessionbuttons, text="+", width=3, command=self.add_session, default=tk.ACTIVE, state="disabled")
        self.add_button.config(**self.timebutton_style)
        self.add_button.grid(row=1, column=1)
        self.del_button = tk.Button(sessionbuttons, text="-", width=3, command=self.remove_session, default=tk.ACTIVE, state="disabled")
        self.del_button.config(**self.timebutton_style)
        self.del_button.grid(row=1, column=2, padx=(0,10))
        self.up_button = tk.Button(sessionbuttons, text="Ʌ", width=3, command=lambda: self.move(-1), default=tk.ACTIVE, state="disabled")
        self.up_button.config(**self.timebutton_style)
        self.up_button.grid(row=1, column=3)
        self.down_button = tk.Button(sessionbuttons, text="V", width=3, command=lambda: self.move(1), default=tk.ACTIVE, state="disabled")
        self.down_button.config(**self.timebutton_style)
        self.down_button.grid(row=1, column=4)
        return self.spin_seconds  # initial focus

    def add_session(self):
        try:
            img_togsess_value = int(self.img_togsess.get() or 0)
        except ValueError:
            img_togsess_value = 0

        try:
            minutes_input = int(self.spin_minutes.get() or 0)
        except ValueError:
            minutes_input = 0

        try:
            seconds_input = int(self.spin_seconds.get() or 0)
        except ValueError:
            seconds_input = 0

        # Convert total seconds to minutes and seconds
        minutes, seconds = divmod(minutes_input * 60 + seconds_input, 60)

        # Format the session string based on the minutes and seconds
        if minutes > 0 and seconds == 0:
            session_str = f"{img_togsess_value} pics for {minutes}m"
        elif minutes == 0 and seconds > 0:
            session_str = f"{img_togsess_value} pics for {seconds}s"
        elif minutes > 0 and seconds > 0:
            session_str = f"{img_togsess_value} pics for {minutes}m {seconds}s"
        else:
            # If both minutes and seconds are missing or 0, use 0s in the string
            session_str = f"{img_togsess_value} pics for 0s"

        # Append session string to the session list
        self.sessionlist.append(session_str)

        # Update the session string representation
        self.sessionString.set(self.sessionlist)

        # Select the newly added session in the session box
        self.sessionbox.selection_set(len(self.sessionlist) - 1)


    def remove_session(self):
        selected_index = self.sessionbox.curselection()
        if not selected_index:
            return

        selected_index = selected_index[0]
        self.sessionlist.pop(selected_index)
        self.sessionString.set(self.sessionlist)

        if selected_index < len(self.sessionlist):
            self.sessionbox.selection_set(selected_index)
        elif len(self.sessionlist) > 0:
            self.sessionbox.selection_set(len(self.sessionlist) - 1)

    def move(self, num):
        #num is -1 for up and 1 for down
        self.idxs = self.sessionbox.curselection()
        if not self.idxs:
            return
        for pos in self.idxs:
            text=self.sessionbox.get(pos) #gets text
            self.sessionbox.delete(pos) #removes text from list box
            self.sessionlist.pop(pos)  #removes text from list
            if pos==len(self.sessionlist) and num==1: pos=-1; #sets at beginning if at bottom and going down
            if pos==0 and num==-1: pos=len(self.sessionlist)+1; #sets at end if at top and going up
            self.sessionbox.insert(pos+num, text) #inserts into list box
            self.sessionlist.insert(pos+num, text) #inserts into list
            self.sessionbox.selection_set(pos+num)

    def toggle_togsess(self):
        if self.togsess_mode.get():
            self.togsess_checkbox.configure(fg="#2c2c2c", bg="#b8b8b8")
            self.img_togsess.config(state = 'normal')
            self.sessionbox.config (state = 'normal')
            self.add_button.config (state = 'normal')
            self.del_button.config (state = 'normal')
            self.up_button.config  (state = 'normal')
            self.down_button.config(state = 'normal')
        else:
            self.togsess_checkbox.configure(**self.timebutton_style)
            self.img_togsess.config(state = 'disabled')
            self.sessionbox.config (state = 'disabled')
            self.add_button.config (state = 'disabled')
            self.del_button.config (state = 'disabled')
            self.up_button.config  (state = 'disabled')
            self.down_button.config(state = 'disabled')

    def set_spinboxes_from_selection(self, event=None):
        index = self.timebox.curselection()
        if not index:
            return
        time_string = self.timebox.get(index)
        if 'm' in time_string:
            minutes = int(time_string.replace('m', ''))
            seconds = 0
        elif 's' in time_string:
            minutes = 0
            seconds = int(time_string.replace('s', ''))
        else:
            # Handle a possible unknown format
            self.bell()  # ring the bell to signal an error
            return

        self.spin_minutes.delete(0, tk.END)
        self.spin_minutes.insert(0, minutes)
        self.spin_seconds.delete(0, tk.END)
        self.spin_seconds.insert(0, seconds)
        
    def buttonbox(self):
        box = tk.Frame(self, bg=self.time_background)
        self.ok_button = tk.Button(box, text="Apply", width=16, command=self.ok, default=tk.ACTIVE)
        self.ok_button.config(**self.timebutton_style)
        self.ok_button.pack(side=tk.TOP, ipadx=5, pady=5)
        box.pack()

    def apply(self):
        minutes = int(self.spin_minutes.get())
        seconds = int(self.spin_seconds.get())
        self.result = minutes * 60 + seconds

    def validate(self):
        try:
            minutes = int(self.spin_minutes.get())
            seconds = int(self.spin_seconds.get())
            return True
        except ValueError:
            self.bell()
            return False

class ImageViewer:
    def __init__(self, root):
		#init methods codes
	def select_folder(self):
		#select folder
	def pause_timer(self):
    def set_timer_interval(self):
        self.root.attributes("-topmost", False)
        
        dialog = CustomTimeDialog(self.root)
        interval = dialog.result
        if interval:
            if interval < 1:  # Check if interval is less than 1 second
                interval = 1
            self.timer_interval = interval * 1000  # Convert to milliseconds
            self.set_timer_interval = self.timer_interval  # Store the set timer interval
        self.root.lift()  # Bring the main window to the top
        self.root.focus_force()  # Give focus to the main window
        
        minutes = self.timer_interval // 60000
        seconds = (self.timer_interval % 60000) // 1000
        
        if self.timer_interval <=59000:
            self.timer_label.config(text=f"{seconds}")
        else :
            self.timer_label.config(text=f"{minutes:d}:{seconds:02d}")

        self.root.attributes("-topmost", True)
    def update_timer(self):
        # Calculate minutes and seconds left
        minutes = self.timer_interval // 60000
        seconds = (self.timer_interval % 60000) // 1000

        if self.timer_interval <=59000:
            self.timer_label.config(text=f"{seconds}")
        else :
            self.timer_label.config(text=f"{minutes:d}:{seconds:02d}")
        
        if self.vol:
            if self.timer_interval == 3000:
                wave_obj = sa.WaveObject.from_wave_file(self.beep1)
                play_obj = wave_obj.play()
                play_obj.wait_done()
            if self.timer_interval == 2000:
                wave_obj = sa.WaveObject.from_wave_file(self.beep1)
                play_obj = wave_obj.play()
                play_obj.wait_done()
            if self.timer_interval == 1000:
                wave_obj = sa.WaveObject.from_wave_file(self.beep1)
                play_obj = wave_obj.play()
                play_obj.wait_done()
            if self.timer_interval == 0:
                wave_obj = sa.WaveObject.from_wave_file(self.beep2)
                play_obj = wave_obj.play()
                play_obj.wait_done()

        self.timer_interval -= 1000
            
        if self.timer_interval < 0:
            self.timer_interval = self.set_timer_interval  # Use the stored set timer interval
            self.next_image()
    def next_image(self, event=None):
        if self.image_folder != "":
            if len(self.image_files) == len(self.history):
                self.history_index = (self.history_index + 1) % len(self.image_files)
            else:
                self.add_image_to_history()
            self.display_image()
                # Reset the timer interval to the stored set timer interval
        self.timer_interval = self.set_timer_interval
    def previous_image(self, event=None):
        if self.image_folder != "":
            if self.history_index > 0:
                self.history_index -= 1
                self.display_image()
        self.timer_interval = self.set_timer_interval  # Use the stored set timer interval
		
class image viewer has a set_timer_interval that calls the entire timer window.

the session box have 3 values, [order in the list, number of picture to be shown, and timer interval to be set]

i want it so that when self.togsess_mode is off or false, it uses the minute and seconds like it is but when it's on or true, and i press apply, the image viewer counts the image shown as the first image, and the timer is being set by the value in the sessionbox items instead of just the minutes and seconds spinbox.

so according to the example sessionlist, it sets the timer according to the first item for a few images (in the example its 5 images for 30s), then for the next number of images it follows the next item on the session list, setting the timer interval for a set of determined number of following images. then i can edit the session using the session box. when the session reaches it's end and it has shown the last item for the determined time, i want it to just run pause_timer method to pause the timer, and reset the image count to run the session again when i press start. keep it concise. lists what i need to change, give the full changed methods

To modify the `ImageViewer` class such that it can support sessions based on `self.togsess_mode`, you'll need to make changes to the `set_timer_interval`, `next_image`, and potentially other related methods.

Here are the changes you should make:

1. Add attributes to store session information in `ImageViewer.__init__`:
   ```python
   def __init__(self, root):
       # ... your existing initialization code ...
       self.session_active = False
       self.session_index = 0
       self.session_image_count = 0
   ```

2. Modify `ImageViewer.set_timer_interval` to handle session mode:
   ```python
   def set_timer_interval(self):
       # ... your existing code before displaying the dialog ...

       dialog = CustomTimeDialog(self.root)
       if dialog.result is not None:
           self.session_active = self.togsess_mode.get()
           if self.session_active:
               self.process_session(dialog.sessionString.get())

       # ... your existing code after dialog.result is checked ...

   def process_session(self, session_list):
       self.current_session_list = []
       for session_str in session_list:
           num_pics, time_str = session_str.split(' pics for ')
           minutes, seconds = self.parse_time(time_str)
           self.current_session_list.append((int(num_pics), minutes * 60 + seconds))
       self.session_index = 0
       self.session_image_count = self.current_session_list[0][0]

   def parse_time(self, time_str):
       splits = time_str.split('m')
       if len(splits) > 1:
           minutes = int(splits[0])
           seconds = int(splits[1].replace('s', ''))
       else:
           minutes = 0
           seconds = int(time_str.replace('s', ''))
       return minutes, seconds
   ```
   
3. Modify `ImageViewer.next_image` to support switching between session intervals:
   ```python
   def next_image(self, event=None):
       # ... your existing code in next_image ...

       if self.session_active:
           self.session_image_count -= 1
           if self.session_image_count <= 0:
               self.session_index = (self.session_index + 1) % len(self.current_session_list)
               self.session_image_count, interval = self.current_session_list[self.session_index]
               self.timer_interval = interval * 1000  # Setting the new interval from session
           else:
               self.timer_interval = self.set_timer_interval
       else:
           self.timer_interval = self.set_timer_interval
   ```

4. Modify `ImageViewer.pause_timer` to reset session when it's paused:
   ```python
   def pause_timer(self):
       # ... your existing code in pause_timer ...
       if self.session_active:
           self.session_index = 0
           self.session_image_count = self.current_session_list[0][0]
           self.timer_interval = self.current_session_list[0][1] * 1000
   ```
   
5. Modify `CustomTimeDialog.apply` to handle session mode:
   ```python
   def apply(self):
       # ... your existing code in apply ...
       if not self.session_active:  # Only set minutes and seconds if not in session mode
           self.result = minutes * 60 + seconds
   ```

You can modify the existing methods `update_timer`, `previous_image`, etc., following a similar pattern as `next_image`, to ensure correct behavior when sessions are active.

These code snippets provide the essential changes needed to integrate session-based timing in the `ImageViewer` class. Once integrated, when `togsess_mode` is enabled and you apply changes from the `CustomTimeDialog`, the image viewer should follow the session intervals you’ve set up rather than the individual time intervals. When the session ends, it calls `pause_timer` to stop the timer and prepares to restart the session when you start the timer again.
give me the update_timer and previous_image

Here are the modified `update_timer` and `previous_image` methods for the `ImageViewer` class to incorporate the session functionality:

```python
    def update_timer(self):
        if self.session_active:
            # The timer interval is set by the session, do not decrement
            interval = self.current_session_list[self.session_index][1]
            self.timer_interval = interval * 1000
        else:
            # Non-session mode, decrement the timer as usual
            self.timer_interval -= 1000
        
        # Update the display
        minutes, seconds = divmod(self.timer_interval // 1000, 60)
        self.timer_label.config(text=f"{minutes:d}:{seconds:02d}")
        
        # Play sound notifications if volume is enabled (vol attribute assumed)
        if self.vol and self.timer_interval in [3000, 2000, 1000, 0]:
            sound_file = self.beep1 if self.timer_interval > 0 else self.beep2
            wave_obj = sa.WaveObject.from_wave_file(sound_file)
            play_obj = wave_obj.play()
            play_obj.wait_done()

        # Check if we need to switch to the next image/session step
        if self.timer_interval <= 0:
            if self.session_active:
                self.next_session_step()
            else:
                self.next_image()

    def next_session_step(self):
        """Advances to the next step in the session mode."""
        self.session_index = (self.session_index + 1) % len(self.current_session_list)
        self.session_image_count, interval = self.current_session_list[self.session_index]
        self.timer_interval = interval * 1000  # Setting the new interval from session
        self.next_image()

    def previous_image(self, event=None):
        if self.image_folder != "" and self.history_index > 0:
            self.history_index -= 1
            self.display_image()

        # When session is active, control the flow based on session settings
        if self.session_active:
            current_count, _ = self.current_session_list[self.session_index]
            # Move to the previous session step, if it's the first image in the current session step
            if self.session_image_count == current_count:
                if self.session_index > 0:
                    self.session_index -= 1
                else:
                    self.session_index = len(self.current_session_list) - 1
                _, interval = self.current_session_list[self.session_index]
                self.session_image_count = self.current_session_list[self.session_index][0]
                self.timer_interval = interval * 1000
            else:
                self.session_image_count += 1
                _, interval = self.current_session_list[self.session_index]
                self.timer_interval = interval * 1000
        else:
            # For regular (non-session) mode, simply reset the timer interval
            self.timer_interval = self.set_timer_interval
``` 

These methods now account for the `session_active` flag. `update_timer` ensures that the correct time interval is used according to the active session. When the time runs out (`self.timer_interval <= 0`), it moves to the next session step by calling `next_session_step`.

`previous_image` has been updated to handle session steps correctly, changing the session step and image count if the user navigates to the previous image during a session. If the session mode is not active, the regular behavior is preserved.

Make sure to integrate these methods into your `ImageViewer` class and adjust them if needed to fit the rest of your application logic.
give me the apply methods again

The `apply` method in the `CustomTimeDialog` class needs to handle the session mode as the `ImageViewer` class uses session details when `togsess_mode` is enabled. Below is the updated method based on the previous responses and integrating the session logic:

```python
    def apply(self):
        if self.togsess_mode.get():
            # When session mode is enabled, the result needs to store the session detail strings
            self.result = self.sessionString.get()
        else:
            # When session mode is disabled, simply store the total time in seconds
            try:
                minutes = int(self.spin_minutes.get())
                seconds = int(self.spin_seconds.get())
                self.result = minutes * 60 + seconds
            except ValueError:
                # If the values in the spinboxes aren't integers, set the result to None
                self.result = None
```

This updated `apply` method sets `self.result` depending on the state of `togsess_mode`:
- If `togsess_mode` is true, it stores the entire session list which can then be processed by the `ImageViewer` to set up the session intervals.
- If `togsess_mode` is false, it calculates the total time in seconds from the minutes and seconds spinboxes and stores that in `self.result`.

You should integrate this `apply` method into the `CustomTimeDialog` class in your application and adjust the `ImageViewer` or other parts of your code to handle the `self.result` appropriately based on what `apply` returns.

if interval < 1:  # Check if interval is less than 1 second
       ^^^^^^^^^^^^
TypeError: '<' not supported between instances of 'str' and 'int'

The error indicates that you're comparing a string (`interval`) with an integer (1), which raises a TypeError. It seems that in your case, `interval` could sometimes be a string representing the session details rather than a numerical value. You'll need to ensure that `interval` is of a proper numerical type before performing comparisons.

To prevent this error, update your `ImageViewer.set_timer_interval` method:

Before you check if `interval` is less than 1, make sure to convert it to an integer if `self.session_active` is `False`. If `self.session_active` is `True`, the `interval` variable should not be used directly to set the timer interval. Instead, you should be initiating processing of the session details.

Here's how you might correct the `ImageViewer.set_timer_interval` method:

```python
def set_timer_interval(self):
    self.root.attributes("-topmost", False)
    
    dialog = CustomTimeDialog(self.root)
    if dialog.result is not None:
        self.session_active = self.togsess_mode.get()
        if self.session_active:
            # Process the session details since result is a string
            self.process_session(dialog.result)
            self.timer_interval = self.current_session_list[self.session_index][1] * 1000
        else:
            # Convert the interval to an integer, since result should be a number
            interval = int(dialog.result)

            if interval < 1:  # Check if interval is less than 1 second
                interval = 1
            self.timer_interval = interval * 1000  # Convert to milliseconds
            self.set_timer_interval = self.timer_interval  # Store the set timer interval

    self.root.lift()  # Bring the main window to the top
    self.root.focus_force()  # Give focus to the main window
    
    # The rest of your method here...
```

Make sure to handle both cases in `ImageViewer.set_timer_interval` based on whether `self.session_active` is `True` or `False`.

Update the `CustomTimeDialog.apply` method to return a numerical value for `self.result` when not in session mode to have consistency of data types:

```python
def apply(self):
    if self.togsess_mode.get():
        # When session mode is enabled, the result needs to store the session detail strings
        self.result = self.sessionString.get()
    else:
        # When session mode is disabled, simply store the total time in seconds
        try:
            minutes = int(self.spin_minutes.get())
            seconds = int(self.spin_seconds.get())
            self.result = minutes * 60 + seconds
        except ValueError:
            # If the values in the spinboxes aren't integers, set a default interval (e.g., 1 second)
            self.result = 1
```

Adjust your code according to the logic needed for your specific use case, following the examples given above to ensure you are comparing the same data types.

interval = int(dialog.result)
               ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
ValueError: invalid literal for int() with base 10: "('5 pics for 30s', '5 pics for 1m', '5 pics for 5m')"

The error occurs because when `dialog.result` contains a tuple of session strings, you're trying to convert it to an integer, which is not possible. To resolve this, you'll need to handle `dialog.result` differently based on whether the session is active (`self.togsess_mode.get()` returning `True`).

If the session is active, you should parse the session strings and set the interval based on the first session details. If the session is inactive, you convert `dialog.result` to an integer, as it should contain a numerical representation of the interval in seconds.

Here's how to modify the `ImageViewer.set_timer_interval` to handle these cases correctly:

```python
def set_timer_interval(self):
    self.root.attributes("-topmost", False)
    
    dialog = CustomTimeDialog(self.root)
    if dialog.result is not None:
        self.session_active = dialog.togsess_mode.get()  # Ensure you're accessing the toggled state from the dialog

        if self.session_active:
            # Session mode is active, dialog.result contains the session list
            self.process_session(dialog.result)
            # Assuming process_session updates self.current_session_list with parsed data
            # Set the interval to the first session's time
            self.timer_interval = self.current_session_list[0][1] * 1000
        else:
            # Session mode is inactive, dialog.result should be a numerical value 
            try:
                interval = int(dialog.result)
                if interval < 1:  # Check if interval is less than 1 second
                    interval = 1
                self.timer_interval = interval * 1000  # Convert to milliseconds
            except ValueError:
                # Handle the case where dialog.result is not a numeric value
                # You may want to log an error message or alert the user
                pass

    self.root.lift()  # Bring the main window to the top
    self.root.focus_force()  # Give focus to the main window
    # ... any other code that needs to be run after setting the interval
```

Make sure that in `process_session`, you correctly parse the session list strings and populate `self.current_session_list`. You should create a consistent format for session details (e.g., a list of tuples with the number of pictures and the interval in seconds).

Your `CustomTimeDialog.apply` method should make sure to return the right kind of data, depending on whether session mode is active or not. It should return the session list (unchanged) if the session is active or a numerical value (interval in seconds) otherwise. Here's an example of how `apply` might be structured:

```python
def apply(self):
    if self.togsess_mode.get():
        # In session mode, store the session strings as received from the list
        self.result = self.sessionString.get()
    else:
        # In standard mode, parse and combine minutes and seconds to a single interval in seconds
        try:
            minutes = int(self.spin_minutes.get())
            seconds = int(self.spin_seconds.get())
            self.result = minutes * 60 + seconds  # Total time in seconds
        except ValueError:
            # Fall back to a default interval as needed
            self.result = 1  # Default to 1 second
```

This should ensure the right type of data is being processed, whether in session mode or not, and prevent the `ValueError`.

The error suggests that when trying to unpack the values from `session_str.split(' pics for ')`, the `session_str` does not have the expected format. This could be because the string does not contain the substring `' pics for '`, therefore the `split` method is not able to split it into two parts as expected.

The `CustomTimeDialog.apply` method should not return the result as `self.sessionString.get()` when `self.togsess_mode.get()` is `True` because `self.sessionString.get()` returns a string representation of the list. Instead, it should return a list of session strings.

Here's how you should change the `CustomTimeDialog.apply` method:

```python
def apply(self):
    if self.togsess_mode.get():
        # In session mode, store the list of session strings directly
        self.result = self.sessionlist  # Use the list directly instead of `self.sessionString.get()`
    else:
        # In standard mode, parse and combine minutes and seconds to a single interval in seconds
        try:
            minutes = int(self.spin_minutes.get())
            seconds = int(self.spin_seconds.get())
            self.result = minutes * 60 + seconds  # Total time in seconds
        except ValueError:
            # Fall back to a default interval as needed
            self.result = 1  # Default to 1 second
```

And here's the change you should make to `ImageViewer.set_timer_interval`:

```python
def set_timer_interval(self):
    self.root.attributes("-topmost", False)
    
    dialog = CustomTimeDialog(self.root)
    if dialog.result is not None:
        self.session_active = dialog.togsess_mode.get()  # Ensure you're accessing the toggled state from the dialog

        if self.session_active:
            # Session mode is active, dialog.result contains the list of session strings
            self.process_session(dialog.result)
            # Set the interval to the first session's time
            self.timer_interval = self.current_session_list[0][1] * 1000
        else:
            # Session mode is inactive, dialog.result should be a numerical value 
            interval = dialog.result
            if interval < 1:  # Check if interval is less than 1 second
                interval = 1
            self.timer_interval = interval * 1000  # Convert to milliseconds

    self.root.lift()  # Bring the main window to the top
    self.root.focus_force()  # Give focus to the main window
    # ... any other code that needs to be run after setting the interval
```

And finally, ensure `ImageViewer.process_session` accepts a list of session strings:

```python
def process_session(self, session_list):
    self.current_session_list = []
    for session_str in session_list:
        try:
            num_pics, time_str = session_str.split(' pics for ')
            minutes, seconds = self.parse_time(time_str)
            self.current_session_list.append((int(num_pics), minutes * 60 + seconds))
        except ValueError as e:
            # Handle the error appropriately, e.g., by logging or messaging the user
            pass  # Or whatever error handling you choose
```

This change should ensure that `process_session` receives the correct input—a list of strings, each formatted correctly with `' pics for '` connecting the number of pics and the time interval. This should prevent the `ValueError` related to unpacking.
when i press start, the timer doesnt move

If the timer is not moving when you press start, there might be several reasons why it isn't functioning properly. Let's go through a checklist to diagnose and fix the issue.

**Check 1: Timer Initialization**
Ensure you start the timer by scheduling the method that updates the timer label and decreases the remaining time. If there's a method like `start_timer()` in your `ImageViewer` class, it should schedule the `update_timer` method using the tkinter root window's `after` mechanism.

Example:
```python
def start_timer(self):
    # This method starts the timer by scheduling the update_timer method
    self.timer_running = True
    self.update_timer()
```

**Check 2: Update Timer Method**
The `update_timer` method should call itself repeatedly using the root window's `after` method in the `ImageViewer` class. Make sure there is a check to see if the timer should continue running (e.g., `self.timer_running` flag).

Example update in `update_timer`:
```python
def update_timer(self):
    if self.timer_running:
        # ... timer logic here ...

        # Check to see if the timer should continue and reschedule the update
        if self.timer_interval > 0:
            self.timer_id = self.root.after(1000, self.update_timer)
        else:
            # If the timer has expired, move to the next image/session item
            self.next_session_step()
```

Make sure you cancel the scheduled timer when you pause or stop, for example:
```python
def pause_timer(self):
    if self.timer_id:
        self.root.after_cancel(self.timer_id)
    self.timer_running = False
```

**Check 3: Session Handling**
If your timer involves sessions, ensure that session logic correctly initializes the timer interval when moving to the next step in the session.

Example addition in `next_session_step`:
```python
def next_session_step(self):
    # ... session-next-step logic ...

    # Restart the timer for the new session step
    if self.timer_running:
        self.update_timer()
```

**Check 4: Timer Display Method**
Ensure that the label or display element that shows the timer is being updated after calculating the new time.

**Check 5: Event Binding**
Ensure that the start button is correctly bound to the `start_timer` method.

Example binding:
```python
start_button = tk.Button(self, text='Start', command=self.start_timer)
```

**Check 6: Initial Timer Interval**
Ensure you have an initial value for `self.timer_interval` and `self.set_timer_interval` in the `ImageViewer` class.

Example initialization:
```python
def __init__(self, root):
    # ... other initializations ...
    self.set_timer_interval = 10000  # default to 10 seconds, for instance
    self.timer_interval = self.set_timer_interval
```

**Check 7: Debugging**
Add print statements or logging at various points in the `start_timer`, `update_timer`, `next_session_step`, and any related methods to ensure they are called and the values change as expected.

Once you've gone through these checks and made any necessary changes, the timer should start and update as expected when you press the start button. If it still doesn't work, you may need to look at the specifics of your code and see if there are other logical issues or missing parts that prevent the timer from starting.

after the first session finishes, the timer sets to 15 instead of pausing. when i press pause, then set the timer or session then press apply, the timer is set to 15 on the second image

It seems like the `update_timer` method is not correctly handling the transition between sessions or stopping after the last session finishes. The timer interval is likely being reset to `15` (which I assume might be the default interval you're using somewhere) instead of pausing as intended.

To resolve this issue, let's ensure that:

1. The session handling logic correctly identifies when the last session ends.
2. The timer correctly pauses when the last session ends.
3. `set_timer_interval` and `update_timer` properly apply the session intervals.

Firstly, ensure that you have a way to identify the end of the final session. If the logic loops back to the first session and you want to pause instead, an attribute flag like `self.last_session` could be used to toggle this behavior.

Here's an adjustment to the `next_session_step` and `update_timer` methods to consider the end of sessions, assuming `self.last_session` is a Boolean flag that should be `True` if the timer should pause after the last session:

```python
def next_session_step(self):
    # Move to the next session index, considering loop around
    self.session_index = (self.session_index + 1) % len(self.current_session_list)
    if self.session_index == 0:  # We've looped around
        if self.last_session:
            self.pause_timer()  # Pause the timer if this was the last session
            return
    self.session_image_count, interval = self.current_session_list[self.session_index]
    self.timer_interval = interval * 1000  # Setting the new interval from session
    # Start the next step or image
    self.next_image()

def update_timer(self):
    if self.timer_running:
        if self.timer_interval > 0:
            self.timer_interval -= 1000
            # ... update timer label ...
            self.timer_id = self.root.after(1000, self.update_timer)
        else:
            if self.session_active and not self.last_session:
                # If in session mode and not the last session, move to the next session step
                self.next_session_step()
            else:
                # Otherwise, stop the timer (and maybe activate pause logic)
                self.pause_timer()
```

Remember to assign the `pause_timer` method to command the pause functionality:

```python
def pause_timer(self):
    if self.timer_id:
        self.root.after_cancel(self.timer_id)
    self.timer_running = False
    self.last_session = False  # Reset last session flag, so the timer can be restarted if needed
    # Update the UI to reflect the paused state
```

Finally, review `set_timer_interval` method to ensure it doesn't inadvertently set the timer to `15` unless that's an intended value. If you have a "Start" or "Reset" button, ensure that it correctly re-initializes the session index, session count, or any relevant attributes.

By following these adjustments, your timer should correctly handle session ends and pauses. Make sure to review your code for consistent session and timer handling logic based on this guidance.
give me the update_timer again

Certainly! Below is an updated `update_timer` method that takes into account session handling. This version checks if the timer interval has reached zero and, if so, decides whether to move to the next session or to pause if the last session has ended.

I've also added some comments and placeholder methods that you may need to implement or integrate with your own logic.

```python
def update_timer(self):
    if self.timer_running:
        # If the timer is still meant to be running
        if self.timer_interval > 0:
            self.timer_interval -= 1000  # Decrement the timer by one second (1000 milliseconds)
            # Update the timer label with the new time
            minutes, seconds = divmod(self.timer_interval // 1000, 60)
            self.timer_label.config(text=f"{minutes:d}:{seconds:02d}")

            # Schedule the next call to this method after 1 second
            self.timer_id = self.root.after(1000, self.update_timer)
        else:
            # If the timer has expired
            if self.session_active:
                # Check if we are in a session and if it's the last image of the session
                if self.session_image_count <= 1:
                    self.session_index += 1  # Advance to the next session

                    # Check if we've finished the last session
                    if self.session_index >= len(self.current_session_list):
                        self.pause_timer()  # Pause the timer if this was the last session
                        return  # Exit the method to prevent executing further logic in this call

                    # If not the last session, initialize the next session's image count and interval
                    self.session_image_count, interval = self.current_session_list[self.session_index]
                    self.timer_interval = interval * 1000  # Convert seconds to milliseconds
                    self.next_image()  # Advance to the next image
                else:
                    # Not the last image in the session; just decrement the image count
                    self.session_image_count -= 1
                    self.next_image()  # Advance to the next image
            else:
                # If not in session mode, we simply pause or reset the timer
                self.pause_timer()
```

Please note the following:
- The `pause_timer` method should be implemented to handle pausing the timer. It generally involves canceling the scheduled timer event and possibly updating UI elements.
- Make sure to handle the timer display update to reflect minutes and seconds appropriately.
- The method `next_image` should advance the image viewer to the next image.
- The `timer_label` widget is assumed to be the display element for the timer.

Ensure consistency between `update_timer`, `next_session_step`, and any other methods that control the session and timer functionalities.

Based on the provided `pause_timer` method you've shared and the new requirements, let's merge both concepts into a cohesive `pause_timer` method. This new method should ensure that both the timer is canceled and the session logic is correctly reset, if applicable:

```python
def pause_timer(self):
    # Cancel the scheduled timer update if it's running
    if self.timer_id:
        self.root.after_cancel(self.timer_id)
        self.timer_id = None  # Nullify the reference to the canceled timer
    
    # Mark the timer as not running
    self.timer_running = False
    
    # If there was a session active, reset the session state
    if self.session_active:
        self.session_index = 0  # Reset session index to the start
        self.session_image_count = self.current_session_list[0][0]  # Reset image count for the first session
        self.timer_interval = self.current_session_list[0][1] * 1000  # Reset the timer interval to the first session's duration
    
    # Update the UI to indicate that the timer is paused
```

The `AttributeError` indicates that the `timer_running` attribute is being referenced before it has been defined in the `ImageViewer` object. To fix this, you need to ensure that `timer_running` is initialized in the `__init__` method of the `ImageViewer` class.

Here's how to initialize `timer_running` in the constructor:

```python
class ImageViewer:
    def __init__(self, root):
        # ... your existing initialization code ...
        self.timer_running = False  # Initialize the timer_running attribute
        # ... more initialization code ...
```

Having this initialization in place, you can safely reference `self.timer_running` in the `update_timer` and any other methods that need to check whether the timer is currently active.

Here's the updated `update_timer` method taking into account this initialization:

```python
def update_timer(self):
    if self.timer_running:
        # If the timer is still supposed to be running
        if self.timer_interval > 0:
            self.timer_interval -= 1000  # Decrement the timer by one second (1000 milliseconds)
            # Update the timer label with the new time
            minutes, seconds = divmod(self.timer_interval // 1000, 60)
            self.timer_label.config(text=f"{minutes:d}:{seconds:02d}")

            # Schedule the next call to this method after 1 second
            self.timer_id = self.root.after(1000, self.update_timer)
        else:
            # Timer has expired, handle the end of the interval
            if self.session_active:  # Check if a session is active
                self.next_session_step()  # Proceed to the next session step
            else:
                self.pause_timer()  # Otherwise, pause the timer
```

Note that `self.timer_id` and `self.timer_label` must also be initialized and that `self.timer_interval` and `self.session_active` need to be managed appropriately within the class. You should check the rest of your code to make sure that `timer_running` is set to `True` when the timer starts and `False` when it stops or pauses.

Now when `update_timer` is called, it will check whether `timer_running` is `True` before attempting to update the timer, preventing the `AttributeError`. Remember to initialize all other class attributes used within your methods in the `__init__` method to avoid similar issues.
